name: Milt Gabler
abstract: Milton Gabler (May 20, 1911 – July 20, 2001) was an American record producer, responsible for many innovations in the recording industry of the 20th century. These included being the first person to deal in record reissues, the first to sell records by mail order, and the first to credit all the musicians on the recordings.

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Wilber
about_quotes.evidence: he said 'you know you kids are really something else, I’d like to record you for Commodore.' So this was a great opportunity. I remember we took about six hours to record four titles.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Wilber interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 22, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: Milt Gabler... heard it, and he approached us about doing it for Decca. Both Dave and I decided we were going to do it just the way it was done by Woody Herman... So we did that
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Teddy McRae
about_quotes.evidence: It was an argument going on between -- in Decca. Dave Cavanaugh -- not Dave Cavanaugh, the other fellow, Milt Gabler, you know. He says, 'Hey, we're doing all right. You know, the records are selling.' But I wanted a better sound out of the records that we were doing

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: The others, I believe -- if I recall, were Vic Dickenson, Joe Venuti, Jess Stacey, Milt Gabler and John S. Wilson, eminent music critic of the New York Times, was on the nominating committee

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: The sessions at Jimmy Ryan's ... were presented by Milt Gabler who owned the Commodore Record Shop ... Jack Crystal who worked in Milt's record shop was in charge of the session

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: Interviewer mentions Milt Gabler in relation to Billie Holiday and the Onyx Club, but Interviewee does not mention a personal or professional relationship with Gabler.
